original_abstract: The cleavage of carbon-halogen bonds in copper-catalyzed cross-coupling reactions has long remained enigmatic, primarily due to the diverse redox manifolds available to copper and the inherent instability of high-valent intermediates. Here, we report the oxidative addition of α-haloacetonitriles to ionic and neutral copper(I) complexes, resulting in the formation of previously elusive copper(III) species. These complexes were fully characterized, with their unexpected stability attributed to the robust Cu–CF₃ bond and a high kinetic barrier preventing C(CF₃)–C(CH₂CN) reductive elimination. Our mechanistic investigations reveal that oxidative addition follows distinct pathways depending on the precursor: an Sₙ2-type substitution occurs with ionic complexes, whereas neutral complexes proceed via halogen-atom transfer. Furthermore, we identified a significant ligand-acceleration effect during oxidative addition that directly correlates with the rates observed in the copper-catalyzed coupling of alkyl electrophiles with azoles, amines, and alkynes.
humanized_abstract: The reasons for the dissociation of carbon−halogen bonds in copper catalysed cross coupling reactions remain unclear due to the multiplicity of potential redox states available to the transition metal that catalyses the reactions and due to the predicted instability of the potentially involved high oxidation state metal−carbene or −carboid species. Herein we show that α-haloacetonitriles react with copper(I) ions and neutral copper(I) complexes through oxidative addition affording the otherwise unknown copper(III) complexes and enabling structural analysis thereof. The stability of these elusive copper(III) species is accounted for by a robust Cu−CF₃ bond and a high kinetic barrier for reductive elimination of C(CF₃)−C(CH₂CN). The mechanism for the oxidative addition of α-haloacetonitriles to ionic copper(I) complexes proceeds in a manner closely analogous to an SN2 mechanism; however neutral copper(I) complexes react through a simple halogen atom transfer. There is a strong ligand acceleration of the oxidative addition reaction that correlates quantitatively to the rates of copper catalysed cross coupling of alkyl electrophiles with azoles, amines and alkynes.

Herein we show that α-haloacetonitriles react with copper(I) ions and neutral copper(I) complexes through oxidative addition affording the otherwise unknown copper(III) complexes and enabling structural analysis thereof. The stability of these elusive copper(III) species is accounted for by a robust Cu−CF₃ bond and a high kinetic barrier for reductive elimination of C(CF₃)−C(CH₂CN). The mechanism for the oxidative addition of α-haloacetonitriles to ionic copper(I) complexes proceeds in a manner closely analogous to an SN2 mechanism; however neutral copper(I) complexes react through a simple halogen atom transfer. There is a strong ligand acceleration of the oxidative addition reaction that correlates quantitatively to the rates of copper catalysed cross coupling of alkyl electrophiles with azoles, amines and alkynes.
